caption:
5 Python one-liners that replace 20 lines of code.

If you're still writing for-loops for these, you're wasting time.

Every one of these is built into Python. No pip install.

📌 SAVE this — your code reviews will thank you.

Which one did you not know? Comment 👇

#python #coding #programming #datascience #machinelearning #developer #backend #pythontips #automation #codingtips

slides.title: Python one-liners that replace 20 lines
slides.subtitle: Built-in. No pip install. Just Python.

slides.title: Counter( ) — count anything in one line
slides.desc: Count occurrences, find most common items, subtract counts. One import.
slides.code:
from collections import Counter

words = ['apple', 'banana', 'apple', 'cherry', 'banana', 'apple']

count = Counter(words)
# Counter({'apple': 3, 'banana': 2, 'cherry': 1})

count.most_common(2)
# [('apple', 3), ('banana', 2)]
slides.punchline: Replaced: for loop + dict + sorting. One line.

slides.title: zip(*matrix) — transpose in one line
slides.desc: Flip rows and columns. No numpy. No nested loops.
slides.punchline: The asterisk unpacks. zip pairs them up. Done.

slides.title: The walrus := operator
slides.desc: Assign and use a value in the same expression. Eliminates redundant calls.
slides.code:
# Without walrus — calls len() twice
if len(data) > 10:
    print(f'Too long: {len(data)}')

# With walrus — one call
if (n := len(data)) > 10:
    print(f'Too long: {n}')

# Filter + capture in list comprehension
results = [y for x in data if (y := process(x)) is not None]
slides.punchline: Assign where you check. One expression.

slides.title: defaultdict( ) — no more KeyError
slides.desc: Auto-creates missing keys with a default value. No if-key-exists checks.
slides.code:
from collections import defaultdict

# Group items by category — no if/else needed
grouped = defaultdict(list)
for item in products:
    grouped[item.category].append(item)

# Count — no initialization needed
counts = defaultdict(int)
for word in text.split():
    counts[word] += 1
slides.punchline: KeyError is a choice. Stop choosing it.

slides.title: itertools.chain( ) — flatten anything
slides.desc: Merge multiple iterables into one. No nested loops. No extend().
slides.code:
from itertools import chain

lists = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]]
flat = list(chain.from_iterable(lists))
# [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

# Merge dicts, sets, generators — anything iterable
all_tags = set(chain(
    post1.tags, post2.tags, post3.tags
))
slides.punchline: Flatten any depth. Any type. One line.

slides.title: Python one-liners decoded
slides.question: Which one saved you the most code? 👇
